fact: May 13 is exactly 133 days into the year - that's like counting to 133 Mississippi's without stopping!
In the Northern Hemisphere, this is typically prime time for baby birds to hatch - their tiny peeps fill the air like nature's own alarm clock.
Dandelions are usually going bonkers right now in northern climates, shooting their fluffy seed parachutes everywhere like tiny alien invasions.
The sun often rises around 5:30 AM in many northern places, which means it's already bright when most kids are still drooling on their pillows.
We're in the middle of Taurus season - the stubborn bull of the zodiac who refuses to budge once it sits down.
In Australia and other southern places, leaves are typically turning crispy colors and falling like confetti as autumn kicks in.
This date sits exactly 39 days after the spring equinox in the north - that's enough time for trees to go from naked branches to full green hair.
Earthworms are usually having a field day in the soil right now in northern climates, wiggling around like underground spaghetti as the ground warms up.
When the moon is full around this time, it often glows with a slightly golden tint because of all the pollen floating in the air.
In many temperate places, this is when morning dew gets so heavy it looks like someone sprinkled diamonds all over the grass.
Bees are typically buzzing around like tiny flying maniacs in northern regions because spring flowers are pumping out nectar like sugary gas stations.
The daylight lasts about 14 hours in northern cities, giving kids way more time to play outside before parents start yelling about bedtime.
